coredata.dc:title: Using Ca-doped carbon dots as catalyst to amplify signal to determine ultratrace thrombin by free-label aptamer-SERS method
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Chongning
Fan, Peidi
Liang, Aihui
Jiang, Zhiliang
coredata.dc:description: The highly catalytic Ca-doped carbon dots (CDCa) were prepared by microwave procedure, that exhibit strong catalytic effect on HAuCl4-glucose (GLC) reaction to form gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) with high SERS activity, using Victoria blue B (VBB) as a molecular probe. The SERS intensity at 1615 cm−1 increased linearly with CDCa increasing, due to formation of more AuNPs nanosol substrate as indicator. When thrombin aptamer (Apt) was added in this system, Apt adsorbed on the CDCa surface to inhibit theirs catalytic activity, and the SERS intensity decreased. However, when thrombin (TB) was present, it can bind to Apt to form stable G-duplex of Apt-TB and free CDCa catalyst in the system, and the SERS signal increased linearly. Thus a free-label Apt-SERS quantitative analysis method was developed for ultratrace TB, with a linear range of 0.0058–0.115 nmol/L and a detection limit of 0.0018 nmol/L TB.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ca-doped carbon dots
Catalyst
Thrombin
Free-label aptamer
SERS

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Energy spectra, X-ray diffraction pattern (XRD) and infrared spectra

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Analytical principle of SERS detection of ultratrace thrombin using Ca-doped carbon dots as catalyst to amplify signal.

a: 0.03 nmol/mL Apt +5.2 μg/L CDCa4 + 5.6 μmol/L HAuCl4 + 0.05 mol/L GLC + 0.4 μmol/L VBB, b: a + 0.0115 nmol/L TB; c: a + 0.023 nmol/L TB; d: a + 0.046 nmol/L TB; e: a + 0.069 nmol/L TB; f: a + 0.087 nmol/L TB; g: a + 0.115 nmol/L TB.

a: 0.03 nmol/mL Apt +0.52 μg/L CDCa4 + 5.6 μmol/L HAuCl4 + 0.05 mol/L GLC, b: a + 0.0115 nmol/L TB; c: a + 0.023 nmol/L TB; d: a + 0.046 nmol/L TB; e: a + 0.069 nmol/L TB; f: a + 0.087 nmol/L TB; g: a + 0.115 nmol/L TB.
Electron microscope image of the AuNPs and CDCa4.
a: SEM, 0.03 nmol/mL Apt +5.2 μg/L CDCa4 + 5.6 μmol/L HAuCl4 + 0.05 mol/L GLC + 0.4 μmol/L VBB; b: SEM, a + 0.036 nmol/L TB; c: SEM, a + 0.69 nmol/L TB; d: d:CDCa4; e: TEM, a + 0.69 nmol/L TB.
Energy spectra (a), XRD (b) and infrared spectra (c) of CDCa4.
Particle size distribution of nanoparticles formed by Apt-TB-HAuCl4-GLC-CDCa4 system.
a: 0.03 nmol/mL Apt +5.2 μg/L CDCa4 + 5.6 μmol/L HAuCl4 + 0.05 mol/L GLC + 0.4 μmol/L VBB, b: a + 0.036 nmol/L TB, c: a + 0.69 nmol/L TB.
Comparison of nanocatalysis and Apt inhibition by SERS method.
Analytical characteristics of the nanocatalytic SERS determination of TB.
Comparing of some reported methods for detection of thrombin.

row.entry: An Apt biosensor based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) of CDS.
Based on thrombin to induce the aptamer-modified fluorescent CDs to form a sandwich structure.

The nanosheets distinguished G‑quadruplexes from single-stranded DNA. The binding of Apt to its target results in forming an anti-parallel G‑quadruplex, the CoOOH affinity weakened, resulting in fluorescence recovering.
Coupled carbon dot capped gold nanoflower electrochemiluminescent with aptamer for assay of thrombin.
Exploiting a dual aptamer and triple enzyme mimetic catalytic oxidizing tetramethylbenzidine (TMB).
Electrochemical response of thrombin was obtained by the coulometric FFT admittance voltammetry.

Biomarkers were adsorbed on Samsa-labeled AuNPs and antithrombin was used as a receptor deposited on the capture substrate. These particles were captured by the biorecognition process between the labeled and immobilized receptors.
High sensitivity, low slope of working curve, complicated.
Visual detection base on strip biosensor through aptamer cleavage reaction with enzyme catalytic amplification.
The aptamer adsorbs on the CD surface, lead to a weak catalytic effect. When thrombin is present, the CDs released and the catalysis recovered to cause SERS peak increased linearly.
High sensitivity, big slope of working curve, simple.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Effect of interfering ions on Apt-TB-HAuCl4-GLC-CDCa4-VBB system.

head.ce:title: Using Ca-doped carbon dots as catalyst to amplify signal to determine ultratrace thrombin by free-label aptamer-SERS method

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Ecology of Rare and Endangered Species and Environmental Protection (Guangxi Normal University), Ministry of Education, Guangxi Key Laboratory of Environmental Pollution Control Theory and Technology, Guilin 541004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Ecology of Rare and Endangered Species and Environmental Protection (Guangxi Normal University)
Guangxi Key Laboratory of Environmental Pollution Control Theory and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The highly catalytic Ca-doped carbon dots (CDCa) were prepared by microwave procedure, that exhibit strong catalytic effect on HAuCl4-glucose (GLC) reaction to form gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) with high SERS activity, using Victoria blue B (VBB) as a molecular probe. The SERS intensity at 1615 cm−1 increased linearly with CDCa increasing, due to formation of more AuNPs nanosol substrate as indicator. When thrombin aptamer (Apt) was added in this system, Apt adsorbed on the CDCa surface to inhibit theirs catalytic activity, and the SERS intensity decreased. However, when thrombin (TB) was present, it can bind to Apt to form stable G-duplex of Apt-TB and free CDCa catalyst in the system, and the SERS signal increased linearly. Thus a free-label Apt-SERS quantitative analysis method was developed for ultratrace TB, with a linear range of 0.0058–0.115 nmol/L and a detection limit of 0.0018 nmol/L TB.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots or carbon dots (CDs) are generally considered to be green carbon nanomaterials with small diameter, good stability and excellent fluorescence [1], whose preparation and application are interesting to peoples. Its synthesis procedures include discharging, laser etching, electrochemical oxidation, chemical oxidation, template, hydrothermal and microwave procedures [2–11]. The hydrothermal is commonly used, and the microwave procedure is fast and simple. Both of them have been used to prepare CDs for fluorescence analysis. Liu et al. [12] used glycerol as a carbon source to synthesize blue fluorescent CDs by hydrothermal, and utilized to detect 0.005–1.2 mmol/L Fe3+ by fluorescence quenching, with detection limit of 2.2 μmol/L. Deng et al. [13] established a graphite-like phase carbon nitride quantum dots (g-CNQDs) fluorescence sensor for 0.1–100 nmol/L trinitrophenol (TNP). Aptamer reaction is of high selectivity, and has been labeled by CDs in fluorescence analysis [14,15]. Shorie et al. [14] reported an aptamer functionalized carbon quantum dots (CQDs) fluorescence assay for the detection of 1–105 ng/ml cardiac marker myoglobin. Ling et al. [15] reported an aptamer labeled CDs fluorescent biosensor for the determination of 0.10–5.00 nM dopamine. However, there are no reports about the highly catalytic CDCa prepared by microwave procedure, the CDCa catalytic AuNP reaction and its application in surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) quantitative analysis of trace thrombin by label-free aptamer.

With the development of nanotechnology, the application scope of SERS technology expanded. It has been applied in adsorption catalytic reactions, environmental analysis, food safety, biomedical analysis and other fields [16–18]. The core technology of SERS is substrate preparation [19]. The SERS signal depends not only on analyte concentration but also on the physical properties of the SERS-enhanced substrate, and its reproducibility [20–22]. To improve SERS sensitivity and selectivity, aptamer has been used in SERS detection. Boushell et al. [23] used an aptamer of egg white lysozyme as a capture agent and coupled to dendritic silver nanoparticle substrate for SERS detection with a detection limit of 0.5 μg/mL. Nie et al. [24] used positively-charged spermine-modified silver nanoparticles as reagents to enhance and capture negatively charged aptamers, the aptamer-modified silver nanoparticles were used for capture of malathion, with detection limit of 5 × 10−7 mol/L. Ye et al. [25] developed an aptamer based SERS assay for 0.5 nmol/L adenosine triphosphate by using primer self-generation. Wang et al. [26] used a SERS-based aptasensor to detect 5 nmol/L Hg2+, based on formation of thymidine (T)-Hg-T to cause AgNP aggregation.

Thrombin is a serine protease. When the intima or tissue is damaged, it will be released and activated to produce an activating factor that converts fibrinogen to fibrin and activates platelets, causing platelet aggregation to promote coagulation or hemostasis. Currently, TB is widely used in local rapid hemostasis for various causes of bleeding, such as gastrointestinal bleeding, surgical bleeding, gynecological bleeding, or for intramuscular injection of ultrasound-guided pseudoaneurysm [27]. Therefore, it is of great important to explore new, simple and rapid, sensitive and selective method for thrombin. Nowadays, main methods for the determination of thrombin include spectrophotometry, fluorescence and electrochemical assay [28–31]. Some new aptamer methods have been reported for assay of thrombin. Liang et al. [32] developed a resonance Rayleigh scattering aptamer nanoprobe to detect 0.115–6.93 nmol/L thrombin. Wang et al. [33] presented an aptamer biosensor based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer CDS for thrombin that provided a linear range from 0.5 to 20 nM with a detection limit of 0.18 nM. Xu et al. [34] used thrombin to induce the aptamer-modified fluorescent CDs to form a sandwich structure, the detection limit was 1 nM thrombin. Yang et al. [35] developed an aptasensor of cobalt oxyhydroxide nanosheets for fluorescence detection of 0.5–100 nM thrombin. You et al. [36] reported a carbon dot capped gold nanoflower aptasensor for electrochemiluminescent detection of 0.5 nM–40 nM thrombin, with a detection limit of 0.08 nM. In this paper, the Ca-doped carbon-doped quantum dots were prepared rapidly by microwave irradiation and their catalytic effect on HAuCl4-GLC reaction was studied by SERS detection. Combined with the specific aptamer reaction without label-free, a new SERS method was established for quantitative analysis of 0.0058–0.115 nM TB, using the catalytic reaction to amplify analytical signal.

A model of DXR smart Raman spectrometer (Thermo Company, United States) with laser wavelength of 633 nm, power of 3.0 mW and slit of 50.0 μm; a model of Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Varian Company, United States); a model of TU-1901 double beam UV–visible spectrophotometer (Beijing General Instrument Co., LTD, Beijing, China); a model of S-4800 field emission scanning electron microscope (Hitachi High-Technologies Corporation, Japan/Oxford company, UK), a model of JEOL-2010 high resolution transmission electronmicroscopy (Japan Electronics Co, Ltd. Japan); a model of WX-6000 microwave digestion instrument (Preekem Scientific Instruments Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China); a model of HH-S2 electric constant temperature water bath (Jintan Earth Automation Equipment Factory, Jintan, China); and a model of SYZ-550-type quartz sub-boiling distiller (Jiangsu Crystal Glass Equipment Factory, Suzhou, China) were used.

A 0.1% HAuCl4 (Sinopharm Chemical Group Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China), 0.5 mol/L glucose (GLC), 50 mmol/L NaOH, 10−5 mol/L Victoria blue B (VBB), 3.7 nmol/L thrombin aptamer (Apt) with sequence of TAG CTA TGG AAT TCC TCG TAG GCA ACA CA, 8.66 μmol/L thrombin (T4648-1000, Sigma Co., USA) were used. The analytically pure grade reagents were used, and water was doubly distilled.

A 1 g citric acid was add into 50 mL Teflon reactor containing 10 mL water to dissolve, 0 mg, 50 mg, 100 mg, 200 mg, 300 mg, 400 mg and 500 mg Ca(OH)2 were added respectively, that were marked as CDCa1-CDCa7. Finally, 500 μL of ethylenediamine was added slowly and mixed well. Sealed and placed in a microwave digestion apparatus with pressure of 4.5 atm and temperature of 140 °C for 10 min. After irradiation, the mixture was cooled to room temperature to obtain a brownish yellow solution, and poured into a 50 mL volumetric flask and shaken uniformly to obtain a carbon dots concentration of 0.1 g/L. Before use centrifuge at 10000 rad/s for 10 min, the pH of CDCa solution was adjusted to 7.5 with 150 mmol/L NaOH solution.

A 120 μL 0.37 nmol/L Apt was took into a 5 mL test tube, and TB standard solution and 70 μL 0.1 mg/L CDCa were added. After 15 min, 100 μL 0.1% HAuCl4, 150 μL 0.01 mol/L GLC were added, diluted to 1.5 mL and mixed well. The tube was heated in 75 °C water bath for 20 min. After cooled it to room temperature, a 60 μL 1.0 × 10−5 mol/L VBB was added. The mixture was transferred into a quartz cell to record the SERS spectra. The SERS intensity at 1615 cm−1 (I) and the blank without TB (I 0) were measured, and a value of ΔI = I–I
0 was calculated.

Under the selected conditions, GLC reduces HAuCl4 slowly. After addition of CDCa catalyst, which exhibited catalytic effect on the reaction of HAuCl4-GLC to generate gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) as SERS substrate and indicator, and make the SERS signal linearly enhancing using VBB as molecular probes in the AuNP sol substrate. Upon addition of Apt in the system, the Apt binds to the CDCa surface and inhibits its catalytic activity. When analyte thrombin was added, it binds to Apt to form a stable complex of G-duplex [34], and the CDCa catalyst released. With the increase of the amount of thrombin, the G-duplex in the system increased, and the catalytic performance enhanced due to free CDCa increasing. Thus, the SERS signal enhanced linearly, and a SERS quantitative analysis method for detecting thrombin can be established (Fig. 1 ).

The nanoreaction between GLC and HAuCl4 is slow. The nanoparticle catalysts such as CDs, graphene oxide nanoribbon (GONR), graphene oxide (GO), fullene (C60), AuNP and silver nanoparticle (AgNP) all make the SERS signal enhancing linearly. For the analytical system, it exhibited four strong SERS peaks at 1178 cm−1, 1200 cm−1, 1395 cm−1 and 1615 cm−1. In which, the peak at 1615 cm−1 is the most sensitive and reproducible and was selected for detection of TB. Seven analytical systems of CDCa were examined, the CDCa4 is most sensitive, and was chosen for use (Figs. 2 , S1a–f).

For the Apt-TB-HAuCl4-GLC-CDCa1-7-VBB system, there is a surface plasmon resonance (SPR) absorption peak at about 530 nm that was ascribing to AuNPs. Among them, the CDCa4 analysis system is the most sensitive (Fig. 3 ), so it was chosen to determine thrombin, but the sensitivity is lower than the SERS. With other CDCas (s = 1, 2, 3, 5, 6, 7) instead of CD4, thrombin can also be detected by the absorption method (Fig. S2a–f).

For the Apt-TB-HAuCl4-GLC-CDCa4 system, the scanning electron microscope (SEM) images show that the AuNP reaction is slow and the produced AuNPs were rare with average size of 50 nm (Fig. 4a). After adding thrombin, CDCa4 is released and the catalytic action is activated to produce a large amount of AuNPs with a size of 20 nm (Fig. 4b). As TB increasing due to the catalytic activity of the system increasing, the amount of produced AuNPs increased gradually in the solution (Fig. 4c). In addition, the SEM and transmission electron microscope (TEM) image of CDCa4 were recorded, the both exhibited that CDCa4 is in spherical shape with an average size of 15 nm (Fig. 4d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Energy spectra, X-ray diffraction pattern (XRD) and infrared spectra
ce:section.ce:para: From the energy spectrum, the mass fraction of each element at the analysis point can be obtained. From the graph, the main elements of the CD are C, O, N and Ca, with Ca(OH)2 added, the content Ca gradually increased, and the C and N contents generated by CDCa4 were high (Fig. 5a, Fig. S3a–b), the Ca-doping levels in CDCa3, CDCa4 and CDCa5 were 1.07, 1.98 and 2.14% (by weight, percentage). To obtain the XRD and IR, the more CDCa4 powder was prepared by vacuum drying freezer. From the XRD of CDCa4 (Fig. 5b), it can be seen that CDCa4 has a wide diffraction peak at 22.8 degrees, which is consistent with the (002) plane of carbon, indicating that CDCa4 has defects and belong to amorphous carbon point. The CDCa4 powder was mixed with a small amount of KBr and ground evenly in an agate mortar, and was pressed by a tablet press that were scanned by infrared spectrometer to obtain the spectra, using pure KBr as reference. The infrared spectra (Fig. 6 ) show that there were the absorption peak at 3785 cm−1 ascribing to OH stretching vibration, the peak at 3424 cm−1 ascribing to OH and NH stretching vibration, the peak at 2346 cm−1 ascribing to NH stretching vibration, the peak at 1672 cm−1 ascribing to CO stretching vibration or CC conjugate structure stretching vibration, the peak at 1385 cm−1 ascribing to C and O 2-symmetric stretching vibration, the peak at 1047 cm−1 ascribing to CO stretching vibration, the peak at 677 cm−1 ascribing to OH surface bending, and the peak at 656 cm−1 ascribing to CCH bending vibration.

Fig. 6 shows the particle size distribution of AuNPs generated by the Apt-TB-HAuCl4-GLC-CDCa4 system reaction. In the blank, the average particle size is 230 nm due to being aggregations of AuNPs. When the thrombin increased, the AuNPs increased, and the average particle size increased due to the AuNPs aggregations enhancing, with an average diameter of 420 and 440 nm for the 0.036 nmol/L TB and 0.69 nmol/L TB systems respectively. The particle size is larger than the TEM size, due to being aggregation of AuNPs in the solution.

The influence of various factors on the SERS intensity (ΔI) was studied. When the other reaction factors held constant in the system, we changed one factor to maximize the SERS intensity. When the concentration of HAuCl4 is 5.6 μmoL/L, the system ΔI reaches the maximum value (Fig. S4a). In the same way, the Apt concentration is 0.03μmoL/L (Fig. S4b). Nanosol catalyst stability is vital to quantitative analysis. And the prepared CDCa4 sol stability was examined by fluorescence and SERS methods due to its exhibition of fluorescence and catalysis. The value of F460nm of the fluorescence spectrum of CDCa4 at different times and the SERS intensity I1615cm−1 of its catalytic system were measured. The both relative standard deviations (RSD) were 6% and 10% (Table 2 ). This shows that the prepared CDCa4 has good stability. The effect of CD concentration was examined, results show that 3.9 μg/L CDCa1 (Fig. S4c), 3.9 μg/L CDCa2 (Fig. S4d), 3.9 μg/L CDCa3 (Fig. S4e), 0.39 μg/L CDCa4 (Fig. S4f), 5.2 μg/L CDCa5 (Fig. S4 g), 260 μg/L CDCa6 (Fig. S4h) and 260 μg/L CDCa7 (Fig. S4i), giving big SERS intensity, were chosen for use. The optimal GLC concentration is 0.05 mol/L (Fig. S4j). VBB is a sensitive molecular probe, and a 0.4 μmol/L VBB was selected for use. Under different reaction temperature, the reaction is different. When the water bath temperature is 75 °C, the system ΔI1615 cm−1 reaches the maximum value (Fig. S4k), so a reaction temperature of 75 °C was chosen. When the reaction time was 20 min, the system ΔI1615 cm−1 reached the maximum value (Fig. S4l), a reaction time of 20 min was selected.

The interference of coexisting substances on the SERS detection of 0.1 nmol/L TB was investigated by the experimental method. The results (Table 5 ) show that when the relative error is within ±10%, the negative and cationic ions at level of 6000 nmol/L–0.1 mmol/L have no influence on the SERS quantitative analysis of TB, which indicating that the SERS method has a good selectivity.

A 3.8% sodium citrate solution was added in fresh blood at a ratio of 1:10, centrifuged at 3500 rpm for 30 min. Added CaCl2 solution to a final concentration of 0.1 mol/L, activated for 2 h at rest and diluted to obtain the sample solution. The thrombin content was determined according to the SERS analytical procedure. The SERS analytical results are agreement with that of enzyme catalytic spectrophotometry (ECS) [40]. Then we added different amounts of thrombin to plasma for standard recovery tests. The relative standard deviation (RSD) was between 1.4% and 9.5%, and the recovery was 90% and 106% (Table 6 ).

CDCa can be used as a catalyst to enhance the reduction of HAuCl4 by GLC. With the increase of CDCa, the reaction is significantly enhanced. The produced gold nanoparticles increased in the system, and VBB as probe molecule has a high SERS effect at 1615 cm−1. The Apt can bind to CDCa and inhibit its catalytic action. When TB is present, it specifically binds to the Apt to form stable G-duplex and free CDCa, and the SERS peak increased linearly at 1615 cm−1 due to its catalytic effect being activated. Based on this, a novel SERS quantitive analysis method for the determination of TB was established, with wide linear range of 0.0058–0.115 nmol/L, high sensitivity, good selectivity and accuracy, and can be applied to detection of trace TB in real sample.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21767004, 21667006).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.msec.2019.02.080.
